content_en:
Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Ali al-Mas'udi was born in Baghdad, probably in the late third century AH (late ninth century CE). He claimed descent from the Companion of the Prophet, Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud, from which his scholarly name derives. His early education was in Baghdad, where he studied Islamic sciences, theology, philosophy, and the natural sciences in one of the most intellectually vibrant cities of the medieval world. He mastered Arabic, possibly learned Persian, and engaged with the Greek philosophical and scientific tradition through the translation movement that had transformed Baghdad's intellectual life.

Al-Mas'udi was above all a traveler and an observer. His life was defined by an extraordinary career of travel across the Islamic world and beyond, driven by an insatiable curiosity about human societies, natural phenomena, and historical change. He traveled to Persia, India, Ceylon (Sri Lanka), China, East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, Egypt, Syria, and the Byzantine borderlands. Everywhere he went, he observed, questioned, and recorded: the political structures he encountered, the religious practices he witnessed, the geographical features he traversed, and the historical traditions he collected from local informants.

This intellectual formation — combining the formal learning of Baghdad with direct observation across a vast geographic range — shaped the distinctive character of his historical writing. He was skeptical of armchair scholarship and insisted on the importance of firsthand knowledge. He criticized historians who wrote about distant lands and peoples without having traveled to them, and he saw his own travels as an epistemological foundation for his historical claims.

He died in Egypt around 345-346 AH (956-957 CE), leaving behind a body of work that makes him one of the most original historical and geographical thinkers of the classical Islamic world. His method of combining direct observation with wide reading from earlier sources — and his willingness to acknowledge what he did not know or had not personally verified — set him apart from many medieval historians who drew exclusively on written traditions. This empirical instinct, combined with a genuine literary gift, explains why Muruj al-Dhahab retained readers and admirers across the centuries when many more voluminous historical works fell into obscurity.
content_id:
Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Ali al-Mas'udi lahir di Baghdad, kemungkinan sekitar tahun 280–283 H (893–896 M), dari keluarga yang secara historis berhubungan dengan Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (ra), salah satu sahabat utama Nabi ﷺ. Meskipun tanggal lahirnya tidak pasti, apa yang jelas adalah bahwa beliau dilahirkan ke dalam lingkungan intelektual yang luar biasa: Baghdad Abbasiyah akhir abad ke-9 adalah salah satu pusat produksi dan sirkulasi pengetahuan paling aktif di dunia.

Al-Mas'udi dikenal terutama sebagai ahli geografi dan sejarawan, tetapi labelnya menyesatkan karena terlalu sempit. Beliau adalah salah satu dari polimaths besar tradisi Arab-Islam — seseorang yang pengetahuannya mencakup sejarah, geografi, teologi, filsafat, ilmu alam, dan sastra. Komitmen intelektualnya terhadap pengalaman langsung sebagai sumber pengetahuan membedakannya dari banyak sarjana sezamannya yang mengandalkan teks-teks yang diterima.

Ciri paling khas dari al-Mas'udi sebagai seorang sarjana adalah komitmennya terhadap perjalanan sebagai metode penyelidikan. Beliau melakukan perjalanan secara luas ke wilayah-wilayah yang beliau tulis — Irak, Persia, India, pantai timur Afrika, Syria, Mesir, dan tempat-tempat lain — dan menulis berdasarkan apa yang beliau amati secara langsung serta apa yang beliau pelajari dari orang-orang berpengetahuan lokal. Pendekatan ini memberikan karya-karyanya kualitas yang hidup yang membedakannya dari banyak geografi dan sejarah Arab yang disusun dari sumber-sumber tekstual saja.

Kematian al-Mas'udi terjadi di Fustat (Kairo) pada tahun 345–346 H (956 M), yang berarti beliau berusia sekitar enam puluhan saat meninggal dunia. Karya produktifnya — lebih dari tiga puluh judul dilaporkan, meskipun sebagian besar hilang — menggambarkan seorang sarjana yang menulis terus-menerus sepanjang kehidupan dewasanya. Kehilangan ini sangat disayangkan karena karya yang masih ada, terutama Muruj al-Dhahab, hanya memberikan gambaran sekilas tentang wawasan intelektual yang tampaknya luar biasa dari seorang pemikir yang benar-benar istimewa.

Pentingnya al-Mas'udi bagi pelajar sejarah Islam tidak dapat dilebih-lebihkan. Beliau hidup pada periode di mana peradaban Abbasiyah berada pada titik puncaknya sebelum kemundurannya, dan beliau menulis dari dalam peradaban itu. Muruj al-Dhahab menyediakan jendela ke dunia intelektual dan fisik Islam abad ke-10 yang tidak dimiliki oleh sumber-sumber lain, dan metode penyelidikan empiris al-Mas'udi menjadikan karya tersebut sumber yang berharga bahkan ketika detail-detailnya tidak dapat diverifikasi secara independen.
content_ar: Abu al-حسن علي ibn al-Husayn ibn علي al-Mas'udi was born in Baghdad, probably in the late ثالثاً century AH (late تاسعاً century CE). He claimed descent from the Companion of النبي ﷺ, Abd الله ibn Mas'ud, from which his العلمي name derives. His early education was in Baghdad, where he studied Islamic sciences, theology, philosophy, and the natural sciences in one of the most intellectually vibrant cities of the medieval الدنيا. He mastered Arabic, possibly learned Persian, and engaged with the Greek philosophical and scientific التراث through the translation movement that had transformed Baghdad's intellectual الحياة. Al-Mas'udi was above all a traveler and an observer. His الحياة was defined by an extraordinary career of travel across the Islamic الدنيا and beyond, driven by an insatiable curiosity about human societies, natural phenomena, and historical change. He traveled to Persia, India, Ceylon (Sri Lanka), China, East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, Egypt, Syria, and the Byzantine borderlands. Everywhere he went, he observed, questioned, and recorded: the political structures he encountered, the religious practices he witnessed, the geographical features he traversed, and the historical traditions he collected from local informants. This intellectual formation — combining the formal learning of Baghdad with direct observation across a vast geographic range — shaped the distinctive الأخلاق of his historical writing. He was skeptical of armchair scholarship and insisted on the importance of firsthand العلم. He criticized historians who wrote about distant lands and peoples without having traveled to them, and he saw his own travels as an epistemological foundation for his historical claims. He died in Egypt around 345-346 AH (956-957 CE), leaving behind a الجسد of work that makes him one of the most original historical and geographical thinkers of the الكلاسيكي Islamic الدنيا. His method of combining direct observation with wide reading from earlier sources — and his willingness to acknowledge what he did not know or had not personally verified — set him apart from many medieval historians who drew exclusively on written traditions. This empirical instinct, combined with a genuine literary الهبة, explains why Muruj al-Dhahab retained readers and admirers across the centuries عندما many more voluminous historical works fell into obscurity.
